title: Try Open Computer Agent, a free AI agent by Hugging Face - LinkedIn
full_text:
Eric Vyacheslav
1y
Report this post
Hugging Face just dropped a free AI agent that uses a computer like a human.
It’s called Open Computer Agent, and it mimics real computer use.
You can run it in your browser no install required.
Are you an AI developer? Check out https://AlphaSignal.ai to get a daily summary of breakthrough models, repos and papers in AI. Read by 200,000+ devs.

The Open Computer Agent is an ambitious leap toward interactive, autonomous AI systems that can handle real user tasks, but it’s far from flawless. From sluggish performance to struggles with CAPTCHAs and complex flows, the current implementation has clear limitations. That said, its open-source foundation is a major win for the community. Despite its rough edges, it’s a promising sandbox for shaping the future of AI-driven automation.

Beyond Transformers: The MoR Revolution is Here! 🚀
The architecture that gave us
ChatGPT and Gemini
might have just met its successor. Meet Mixture of Recursions (MoR), a new model from Google DeepMind that's rewriting the rules of AI.
Check out these specs:
🚀 2x Faster
🧠 50% Less Memory
⚡️ Half the Training Compute
How? MoR works smarter, not just harder. It intelligently decides how much computational power each word actually needs, saving massive resources while boosting performance.
This isn't just an update; it's a potential revolution in how AI reasons and learns. We're entering a new era of leaner, faster, and more efficient intelligence.
Is MoR the future? Let me know what you think! 👇
#AI #Innovation #GoogleDeepMind #MoR #LLM

Google AI Proposes ReasoningBank:
A Strategy-Level I Agent Memory Framework that
Makes LLM Agents Self-Evolve at Test Time - MarkTechPost
How do you make an LLM agent actually learn from its own runs—successes and failures—without retraining?
Google Research proposes ReasoningBank, an AI agent memory framework that converts an agent’s own interaction traces—both successes and failures—into reusable, high-level reasoning strategies.
These strategies are retrieved to guide future decisions, and the loop repeats so the agent self-evolves
https://lnkd.in/gJJY3n8P

Report this post
The performance of every language model is influenced by the balance between model size, computational resources, and efficiency—scaling laws describe how these factors interact for practical deployment.
For many real-world applications, what truly matters is speed, cost, and control. Small Language Models are built to be lighter, faster, and easier to deploy on personal servers, mobile devices, or internal systems.
They're great at summarizing legal documents, analyzing support tickets, or handling specific domain tasks. This shift is about making AI more practical and accessible — where smaller, fine-tuned models become the backbone of efficient, domain-specific AI deployments.

What’s your take on AI as an augmentative tool, not a replacement?

💡 Free AI API Keys — Yes, You Read That Right!
A few days ago, I talked about Ollama models that you can host locally on your machine.
But today, let’s talk about something even more exciting — Free API Keys 🧠
Go to 👉 https://lnkd.in/dsheFGD5
Then simply select “Free” in the model filter — and boom 💥 you’ll find tons of AI models you can use without paying a single penny.
Why this matters:
• You can experiment with different AI models for free.
• Perfect for testing, learning, and building prototypes.
• Some models even offer unlimited tokens — ideal for devs on a budget.
It’s a great way to explore AI APIs without worrying about cost or credit limits.
So go check it out — and thank me later 😁
#AI #FreeAPI #OpenRouter #MachineLearning #Developers #AIDev #OpenSource #TechTips

Vishal Rustagi
8mo
Report this post
If you zoom out on the last 30 years of AI, it’s not just a story of faster models, it’s a story of evolution in intelligence.
We started with rule-based systems machines that could only do what we told them to.
Then came classical machine learning algorithms that started finding patterns on their own.
The deep learning era taught computers to see, speak, and understand giving rise to everything from voice assistants to computer vision.
But the real shift happened with Transformers and LLMs.
For the first time, machines could understand and generate natural language but they still had limits.
Static models. Hallucinations. No real-time grounding.
Then came RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) a bridge between LLMs and the outside world. Models could now pull verified, contextual data to make their answers smarter.
That’s when Tool Use & Function Calling entered enabling LLMs to act, not just answer.
Calling APIs, executing code, retrieving databases suddenly, AI wasn’t just conversational, it was operational.
Now, we’re entering the Agentic Era, where AI systems plan, reason, and collaborate with other agents. They’re not just tools anymore; they’re teammates.
Multi-agent systems where dozens of AI agents coordinate, share memory, and execute complex workflows together.
Every leap in this journey has one thing in common, we’re moving from intelligence in isolation to intelligence in collaboration.
That’s where the real potential (and responsibility) lies.
What excites me most isn’t what AI can do today .
It’s what happens when these systems start working together, seamlessly, like teams do.
Because the future of AI won’t be about one model being smarter.
It’ll be about many systems thinking together.
#Ariedge #Automation #ArtificialIntelligence #AgenticAI #LeadershipInTech #FutureOfWork #AIInnovation

Sebastian Barros
8mo
Report this post
(Almost) Everything You’ve Heard About AI Agents Is a Lie
An AI agent in 2025 is not an autonomous worker. It is a large language model wrapped with a planner, some APIs, memory, and error handling. A probabilistic system pretending to be deterministic.
The math is ruthless. Ten steps at 90% accuracy yield only a 35% chance of success. Benchmarks confirm it: most state-of-the-art agents complete fewer than half of real workflows.
Still, there is value in current AI Agents!
In narrow domains, with clear constraints and human oversight, agents are already useful. They draft SQL queries, triage tickets, handle exceptions, and cut cycle times. This is where they work today.
What enterprises cannot afford is vendor hype.
Deployment requires shadow mode testing, full observability, validation layers, and strict permission boundaries. Without this, you are funding expensive failures.
Agents are fragile orchestrators. Not lies, not magic. Just engineering. This will change in the future as research continues to progress at a rapid pace.
Read the rest here:
https://lnkd.in/gXVn9WSN

Paras Sakarwal
7mo
Edited
Report this post
Debugging your AI agent? You're probably looking at the wrong thing. 🧐
Most of us instinctively focus on the final, flawed output. But that's like treating the symptom and ignoring the disease.
The real failure almost always happens earlier, in the agent's internal Thought process or during a Tool Call. If the reasoning is flawed from the start, the output is guaranteed to be garbage.
🔍 The Fix: Stop staring at the end result. Dive into the Trace.
Your debug process should be:
1️⃣ Find the bad Agent Response.
2️⃣ Scroll up to the immediate Thought or Tool Input that preceded it.
3️⃣ Target your prompt fix or adjustment there.
That's where you'll find the lightning-speed solution.
Debugging an agent means debugging the timeline of its decisions.
Want to streamline this process? Check out how Neatlogs lays out clear, step-by-step traces for faster debugging:
☑️Sample Trace: https://lnkd.in/gs6rDAj6
📘 Docs: https://docs.neatlogs.com
Explore More: https://lnkd.in/g9bxGBHh | https://neatlogs.com
What’s your go-to strategy for debugging AI agents? Share below! 👇
#AI #AIAgents #PromptEngineering #Debugging #crewai

Is MoR the future? Let me know what you think! 👇

Google AI Proposes ReasoningBank:
A Strategy-Level I Agent Memory Framework that
Makes LLM Agents Self-Evolve at Test Time - MarkTechPost
How do you make an LLM agent actually learn from its own runs—successes and failures—without retraining?
Google Research proposes ReasoningBank, an AI agent memory framework that converts an agent’s own interaction traces—both successes and failures—into reusable, high-level reasoning strategies.
These strategies are retrieved to guide future decisions, and the loop repeats so the agent self-evolves

The performance of every language model is influenced by the balance between model size, computational resources, and efficiency—scaling laws describe how these factors interact for practical deployment.
For many real-world applications, what truly matters is speed, cost, and control. Small Language Models are built to be lighter, faster, and easier to deploy on personal servers, mobile devices, or internal systems.
They're great at summarizing legal documents, analyzing support tickets, or handling specific domain tasks. This shift is about making AI more practical and accessible — where smaller, fine-tuned models become the backbone of efficient, domain-specific AI deployments.

Vishal Rustagi
8mo
Report this post
If you zoom out on the last 30 years of AI, it’s not just a story of faster models, it’s a story of evolution in intelligence.
We started with rule-based systems machines that could only do what we told them to.
Then came classical machine learning algorithms that started finding patterns on their own.
The deep learning era taught computers to see, speak, and understand giving rise to everything from voice assistants to computer vision.
But the real shift happened with Transformers and LLMs.
For the first time, machines could understand and generate natural language but they still had limits.
Static models. Hallucinations. No real-time grounding.
Then came RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) a bridge between LLMs and the outside world. Models could now pull verified, contextual data to make their answers smarter.
That’s when Tool Use & Function Calling entered enabling LLMs to act, not just answer.
Calling APIs, executing code, retrieving databases suddenly, AI wasn’t just conversational, it was operational.
Now, we’re entering the Agentic Era, where AI systems plan, reason, and collaborate with other agents. They’re not just tools anymore; they’re teammates.
Multi-agent systems where dozens of AI agents coordinate, share memory, and execute complex workflows together.
Every leap in this journey has one thing in common, we’re moving from intelligence in isolation to intelligence in collaboration.
That’s where the real potential (and responsibility) lies.
What excites me most isn’t what AI can do today .
It’s what happens when these systems start working together, seamlessly, like teams do.
Because the future of AI won’t be about one model being smarter.
It’ll be about many systems thinking together.
#Ariedge #Automation #ArtificialIntelligence #AgenticAI #LeadershipInTech #FutureOfWork #AIInnovation

Sebastian Barros
8mo
Report this post
(Almost) Everything You’ve Heard About AI Agents Is a Lie
An AI agent in 2025 is not an autonomous worker. It is a large language model wrapped with a planner, some APIs, memory, and error handling. A probabilistic system pretending to be deterministic.
The math is ruthless. Ten steps at 90% accuracy yield only a 35% chance of success. Benchmarks confirm it: most state-of-the-art agents complete fewer than half of real workflows.
Still, there is value in current AI Agents!
In narrow domains, with clear constraints and human oversight, agents are already useful. They draft SQL queries, triage tickets, handle exceptions, and cut cycle times. This is where they work today.
What enterprises cannot afford is vendor hype.
Deployment requires shadow mode testing, full observability, validation layers, and strict permission boundaries. Without this, you are funding expensive failures.
Agents are fragile orchestrators. Not lies, not magic. Just engineering. This will change in the future as research continues to progress at a rapid pace.
Read the rest here:
https://lnkd.in/gXVn9WSN

Debugging your AI agent? You're probably looking at the wrong thing. 🧐
Most of us instinctively focus on the final, flawed output. But that's like treating the symptom and ignoring the disease.
The real failure almost always happens earlier, in the agent's internal Thought process or during a Tool Call. If the reasoning is flawed from the start, the output is guaranteed to be garbage.
🔍 The Fix: Stop staring at the end result. Dive into the Trace.
Your debug process should be:
1️⃣ Find the bad Agent Response.
2️⃣ Scroll up to the immediate Thought or Tool Input that preceded it.
3️⃣ Target your prompt fix or adjustment there.
That's where you'll find the lightning-speed solution.
Debugging an agent means debugging the timeline of its decisions.
Want to streamline this process? Check out how Neatlogs lays out clear, step-by-step traces for faster debugging:
☑️Sample Trace: https://lnkd.in/gs6rDAj6
📘 Docs: https://docs.neatlogs.com
Explore More: https://lnkd.in/g9bxGBHh | https://neatlogs.com
What’s your go-to strategy for debugging AI agents? Share below! 👇
#AI #AIAgents #PromptEngineering #Debugging #crewai
